facts_disclosed: Saving for own truck, $85,000 target, $12,000 saved, 2-year plan

turns.content: okay so i been doing math tonight in the sleeper cab and i need to talk this out with someone because my brain is going in circles
I'm here — lay it on me. What kind of math are we talking about?

so right now i drive somebody elses truck right and they pay me per mile which is fine but the company is making money off my labor like they charge the shipper way more than what they pay me and i keep thinking what if i had my own rig you know like be an owner operator and keep all the money instead of making somebody else rich

That's a really common thought among drivers — the owner-operator path. It can be more profitable, but it also comes with a lot more risk and responsibility. What's got you thinking about it seriously now?

i met this guy at a truck stop in meridian mississippi last week and hes an owner operator and he was telling me he clears like 200k gross a year and even after expenses hes taking home way more than me and i was like man thats the dream because right now at 68 cents a mile after taxes im not building anything im just surviving you know

That's a big difference if the numbers work out. $200k gross sounds great, but the key word is "after expenses" — fuel, insurance, maintenance, taxes, permits, those add up fast for owner-operators. Did he break down his actual take-home for you?

he said after everything he keeps about 80 to 90 thousand which is still way more than what im making now and he sets his own schedule and picks his own loads and doesnt answer to nobody which is basically everything i already love about trucking but more of it you know so ive been looking at what it would take to buy a truck
Okay so this is getting real then. What does a decent truck run for if you're buying one to go owner-operator?

so a good used truck like a freightliner or a peterbilt thats maybe 3 or 4 years old with decent miles on it youre looking at about 85 thousand dollars give or take and i been saving up for a while now i got about 12 thousand put away which i know is not a lot but its something you know its a start
Hey, $12,000 saved up is real progress — don't sell yourself short on that. So you're looking at an $85,000 target. That's a $73,000 gap. Are you thinking financing, or saving up the whole amount?

combination probably like if i can get to maybe 25 or 30 thousand for a down payment then finance the rest because the interest rates for truck loans are not terrible if you have good credit and my credit is okay not great but okay and i was listening to this podcast the other day about like building wealth as a blue collar worker and the guy was saying the biggest mistake people make is waiting too long to invest in themselves and i was like thats me im waiting and waiting when i should be making moves

That podcast advice is solid — there's truth to the idea that waiting for the "perfect" moment can hold you back. So with $12k saved and needing around $25-30k for a down payment, what's your timeline looking like? How fast can you save the rest?

so i did the math tonight and if i keep saving like i have been putting away about 600 to 800 a month depending on how many miles i run then in about 2 years i should be at the down payment number and then i can finance the rest and be my own boss like for real not just kind of free like i am now but actually free you know choosing my loads setting my rates building something thats mine

Two years is a realistic timeline, especially if you can stay disciplined with the saving. $600-800 a month over 24 months gets you to $14,400-$19,200 on top of your existing $12,000 — so you'd be in that $26,000-$31,000 range for the down payment. The math works.

Have you looked into what the monthly loan payments would be on the remaining $55-60k?

roughly like 1200 to 1500 a month depending on the rate and term which is a lot but if im making owner operator money it should be manageable and honestly having a payment like that would motivate me to keep hustling you know like theres no calling in sick when you owe 1500 a month on a truck but thats fine because i already dont call in sick anyway mwen fatige sometimes but i still drive

That's a real point — a truck payment is great motivation to stay on the road. And you're right, you're already putting in the hours anyway. The difference is those hours would be building equity in your own business instead of just earning a wage.

What about insurance and maintenance costs as an owner-operator? Those can surprise people.

yeah thats the part that scares me a little because right now the company handles all that and if something breaks its their problem not mine but as an owner operator if the engine goes or the transmission dies thats on me and insurance for a commercial truck is not cheap either but the guy in meridian said you just gotta budget for it and have an emergency fund and i figure if i can save 12 thousand while making company driver money i can definitely save more when im keeping all the revenue you know

That's logical thinking — if you can save on a smaller income, you should be able to save more on a bigger one, as long as you don't let expenses creep up. The emergency fund is crucial though. A lot of owner-operators get caught without a cushion and one big repair puts them under.

It sounds like you've got a solid 2-year plan forming. Are you writing any of this down, or is it all in your head right now?

its mostly in my head and in my notes app on my phone which is a mess but tonight talking it out with you is helping me organize it you know like its real now its not just a daydream its a plan with numbers and a timeline and i know its going to be hard but bondye bon everything worth having is hard and i want naomi to see her dad own something you know not just work for somebody else forever i want her to know that you can come from port au prince with nothing and build something in america thats the whole point right

Man, that's a powerful motivation — showing Naomi what's possible. That kind of "why" is what keeps people going when the saving gets hard or when month 14 feels like forever. You've got the numbers, the timeline, the discipline, and most importantly, a reason that goes way beyond money.

Two years from now, rolling in your own rig — that's going to be a good day. Keep running those numbers and keep stacking that savings. You've got this.

appreciate you for real man i needed to hear that alright im going to sleep now because i gotta be in nashville by 8am tomorrow and its already midnight but my brain feels clearer now like i got a direction you know sa k pase goodnight

Goodnight! Get some rest — Nashville by 8am means you need those hours. Your plan sounds solid, and the fact that you're running the numbers instead of just dreaming about it says a lot. Sleep well and drive safe tomorrow. Sa k pase!
